---
title: "Data states"
description: "Advanced: how the data object changes shape as it flows, a view for pipeline authors."
order: 70
---

There are two ways to read the pipeline. [Pipeline
architecture](architecture.qmd) reads it **by stage**, the way an analyst thinks.
This page reads it **by the state of the data**, the way the workflows are
actually organized. The unit of data is not constant: it changes shape as it
flows, and a component only makes sense in one state. If you are writing or
wiring components, this is the view that tells you where a step belongs.

```{mermaid}
%%| label: fig-data-states
%%| fig-cap: "The data object changes shape as it flows. Ingestion produces one multimodal object per sample; the pipeline splits it by modality for per-sample work, concatenates the samples for cohort-wide work, merges the modalities back together, then integrates."
flowchart TD
  classDef step fill:#dae8fc,stroke:#6c8ebf,color:#000
  classDef obj  fill:#f5f5f5,stroke:#aaaaaa,color:#444

  ingested["one multimodal H5MU per sample"]:::obj

  subgraph SS [single-sample state]
    split["split modalities"]:::step --> ssqc["filter + QC<br>per sample, per modality"]:::step
  end

  subgraph MS [multi-sample state]
    concat["concatenate samples<br>adds sample_id"]:::step --> msproc["normalize, select features, PCA<br>per modality"]:::step
  end

  subgraph MG [merged state]
    merge["merge modalities"]:::step
  end

  subgraph IN [integrated state]
    integ["batch correction<br>clusters + UMAP"]:::step
  end

  ingested --> split
  ssqc --> concat
  msproc --> merge
  merge --> integ
```

## Why the state matters

Some operations are only valid in one state. Per-sample work like QC and
filtering reasons about a single sample's own distribution, so it runs in the
**single-sample** state. Normalization, feature selection, and PCA need to see
variation across the whole cohort, so they run only after concatenation, in the
**multi-sample** state. That is what the `_singlesample` / `_multisample`
suffixes throughout the codebase mean: they declare the state a workflow operates
on.

| State | Example workflows | Operates on |
|---|---|---|
| Single-sample | `rna/rna_singlesample`, `prot/prot_singlesample` | one sample, one modality |
| Multi-sample | `rna/rna_multisample`, `prot/prot_multisample` | the concatenated cohort |
| Merged | `process_batches` (via `dataflow/merge`) | all modalities together |
| Integrated | `integration/harmony_leiden`, ... | the corrected embedding |

The `multiomics` workflows (`process_samples`, and `process_batches` for large
cohorts) move data between these states, so you rarely wire the transitions by
hand.

## When you author a component

Before adding a component to a workflow, decide which state it reads: a **single
sample** (so it belongs before concatenation, in a `_singlesample` workflow) or
the **whole cohort** (so it belongs after, in a `_multisample` workflow). That
one question tells you where the component slots in and which assumptions hold
(is `sample_id` present yet? is the data normalized?). See
[Create a pipeline](../contributing/creating-pipelines.qmd) for the mechanics of
wiring one in.
